transcript:
What you're watching is a screen full of confident phrases — "I know," "definitely," "obviously" — condensing out of grey static and then dissolving back into it, over and over, drifting slowly across a black field.

Here's the part that got me: every crystal is secretly tagged with a variable called "was earned" — a coin flip near the top of the code that decides whether that phrase came from a tight, confident source or a diffuse, guessing one. It even sets a wider "source entropy" for the guesses. But look at the render function — that spread value gets multiplied by an easing curve that crushes it to zero by the time the text solidifies. Tight or diffuse, earned or fabricated, they converge on the exact same crisp white letters. The code even says it outright, in a comment: "That's the point."

And down in the corner, barely legible, it's literally counting them for you — "certainties visible," "distribution of truth: unknown" — confessing the trick while still refusing to tell you which words you can believe.
